mappings.rationaleAI: Babylon covers the Old Babylonian Empire under Hammurabi (B.1.d.iii), the Neo-Babylonian Empire of Nebuchadnezzar including the destruction of Jerusalem's Temple (G.2.a), and Babylonian astronomy and mathematics.
The Hittites covers the establishment of the Old Hittite Kingdom in Anatolia (D.2.a.iv) and the Hittite Empire's conflicts with Egypt (E.2.a).
The Epic of Gilgamesh as one of the earliest Sumerian literary works with legendary figures directly addresses early Sumerian literary sources and legendary figures like Gilgamesh (B.1.b.i).
Akhenaten's religious reforms and the cult of Aton directly address the reign of Akhenaton and the cult of the god Aton in the 18th dynasty (E.1.a.ii).
Hatshepsut's reign including her mortuary temple and building programs directly addresses the 18th dynasty's territorial expansion and cultural developments (E.1.a.i).

Cyrus the Great's establishment of the Achaemenid Empire, conquest of Babylon, and liberation of the Jews directly addresses the establishment of Achaemenid Persian rule (G.2.d.ii) and Achaemenid society and culture (G.3.c).
Persepolis as ceremonial capital of the Achaemenid Empire with its monumental architecture directly addresses Achaemenid capitals and culture (G.3.c).
Alexander the Great's conquest of the Persian Empire directly addresses the conquest resulting in the end of Achaemenid rule (G.3.b).
The Sassanid Empire directly addresses extension of Iranian power under the Sasanian dynasty (H.2) including its founding, wars with Rome/Byzantium, and Arab conquest.

The Phoenicians as maritime civilisation with alphabetic writing, the founding of Carthage, and trade directly address Canaanite-Phoenician commercial city-states (F.2.a) and Levantine seafaring trade (E.2.b.i).
The Iron Age beginning around 1200 BC in the Near East directly addresses the era of new states and the beginning of the Iron Age (F).
The Bronze Age collapse around 1200-1150 BCE including the fall of Mycenaean Greece, Hittite Empire, and Sea Peoples directly addresses the end of the Bronze Age in the Aegean (E.5.b).
The Minoan civilisation of Bronze Age Crete with palace complexes, Linear A, and the Thera eruption directly addresses the Minoan civilization (D.2.c.ii).
The Trojan War's archaeological evidence at Hisarlik and relationship to Bronze Age events directly addresses Mycenaean civilization (E.5).
The Tale of Sinuhe from the Middle Kingdom period directly addresses Middle Kingdom Egypt (C.3.a) including political and literary developments.
The origins of writing including cuneiform in Mesopotamia and hieroglyphics in Egypt directly address the invention of cuneiform writing by the Sumerians (B.1.b.i).
The alphabet's history from Proto-Sinaitic and Phoenician origins directly addresses the development of the linear alphabet by the Canaanites (E.2.b.i).
Carthage's destruction covers the rivalry between Rome and Carthage and the Punic Wars resulting in Carthage's destruction (I.5.b).
The Roman Republic covers the Punic Wars and Roman penetration into North Africa — relevant to the destruction of Carthage and Roman conquest (I.5.b/I.5.c).
Thermopylae covers the Persian invasion of Greece under Xerxes — directly relevant to the Achaemenid Empire's attempts to conquer mainland Greece (G.3.a).
Tutankhamun's restoration of traditional polytheism after Akhenaten's reforms directly addresses the eclipse of the 18th dynasty (E.1.a.ii).
